text.fullText: February 17, 2026 Jessica Kramer, Assistant Administrator Oﬃce of Water Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 VIA Federal eRulemaking Portal: https://www.regulations.gov. Re: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Comments of Chelan County Public Utility District on the Proposal to Update the Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certiﬁcation Regulations Dear Assistant Administrator Kramer: Chelan County Public Utility District (Chelan PUD) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) Proposed Rule to update its Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 water quality certiﬁcation regulations. As a hydropower owner and operator, Chelan PUD strongly supports the Proposed Rule because it would revise EPA’s 2023 rule to properly limit the scope of certiﬁcations to discharges in accordance with the statute. If implemented as envisioned, the Proposed Rule would help restore congressional intent under the CWA and appropriately recognize the exclusive authority of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) to issue licenses for the purpose of constructing, operating, and maintaining non-federal hydroelectric dams under the Federal Power Act (FPA). In addition to our own comments, Chelan PUD is a member of both the National Hydropower Association (NHA) and Northwest Hydroelectric Association (NWHA) and support their comments in this Docket. Background Chelan PUD is a Washington State public utility district formed in 1936 by local voters who wanted affordable power for both rural and urban residents. We own and operate two hydroelectric dams on the Columbia River (the Rocky Reach and Rock Island Projects) and one on Lake Chelan licensed by FERC. These three hydropower projects total about 2,000 megawatts of capacity and deliver approximately 1100 average megawatts of clean, renewable energy. For decades, the region has relied on our hydropower system, which generates surplus power used throughout the Northwest after serving our 50,000 local customers. We received new FERC licenses supported by Section 401 water quality COMMISSIONERS: Kelly Allen, Garry Arseneault, Carnan Bergren, Steve McKenna, Randy Smith GENERAL MANAGER: Kirk Hudson Chelan County PUD comments cont. certifications for the Rocky Reach Project in 2009 and the Lake Chelan Project in 2006. The Rock Island Project is seeking a new federal operating license from FERC because its existing license will expire in 2028. As part of that process, Chelan PUD will request a Section 401 water quality certification from the Washington State Department of Ecology. In navigating compliance obligations under the CWA and FPA, two issues stand out. First, the scope of Section 401 influences how agencies and tribes participate in hydropower licensing under other aspects of federal law – particularly FPA Sections 10(a) and 10(j). Second, the interpretation of what constitutes a discharge that is subject to certification for compliance with Sections 301, 302, 303, 306 and 307 of the CWA presents unique challenges in the context of hydropower projects, whose discharges primarily pass water through the project from upstream to downstream, rather than add wastewater or other water to a waterbody. The FPA gives FERC the exclusive authority to issue licenses for the purpose of constructing, operating, and maintaining non-federal hydroelectric dams. Under Section 10(a) of the FPA, FERC is required to assure that a hydropower project is “best adapted to a comprehensive plan of development” of the aﬀected river basin for beneﬁcial uses such as energy generation, water supply, ﬂood control, recreation, and ﬁsh and wildlife. 1 In 1986, the Electric Consumers Protection Act (ECPA) was enacted. It broadened FERC’s responsibilities in the licensing process by requiring it to give “equal consideration to the purposes of energy conservation, the protection, mitigation of damage to, and enhancement of, ﬁsh and wildlife (including related spawning grounds and habitat), the protection of recreational opportunities, and the preservation of other aspects of environmental quality.” 2 However, federal and state agencies each play a deﬁned role in imposing mandatory conditions and prescriptions and making ﬁsh and wildlife recommendations. • Sections 4(e) of the FPA authorizes the Secretaries of the Interior and Agriculture to impose mandatory conditions for projects on federal reservations. FERC must accept these conditions and incorporate them into a new license for the project, without modiﬁcation. 3 • Section 18 of the FPA authorizes the Secretaries of Commerce and Interior to impose mandatory ﬁshway prescriptions on projects. FERC must accept these conditions and incorporate them into a new license without modiﬁcation. 4 • Section 10(j) of the FPA 5 provides federal and state ﬁsh and wildlife agencies the opportunity to recommend to FERC terms and conditions related to the protection, mitigation, and enhancement of ﬁsh and wildlife resources aﬀected by the project. FERC is required to accept section 10(j) recommendations unless it determines they are inconsistent with the purposes and requirements of the FPA. 1 16 U.S.C. § 803(a)(1). 2 16 U.S.C. § 797(e). 3 16 U.S.C § 797(e). 4 16 U.S.C. § 811. 5 16 U.S.C. § 803(j) 2 Chelan County PUD comments cont. • Section 10(a) of the FPA 6 provides federal and state agencies and tribes the opportunity to recommend to FERC license conditions for the comprehensive use of the waterway, such as public recreation, navigation, water supply and other public uses. FERC has discretion to accept, modify, or reject recommendations submitted under Section 10(a). Through the FPA, therefore, federal and state ﬁsh and wildlife agencies have signiﬁcant authorities to provide input into the hydropower licensing process regarding land use and ﬁsh and wildlife. FERC, in turn, is responsible for balancing competing uses by exercising its discretion to accept or reject these recommendations. When Section 401 is interpreted as allowing certifying authorities to impose broad conditions untethered to discharges, it becomes a vehicle to convert discretionary recommendations made through FPA Sections 10(a) and 10(j) into mandatory Section 401 certiﬁcation conditions. That outcome denies FERC its exclusive role and discretion under the FPA and expands Section 401 beyond its discharge-centered statutory function. In practice, this has resulted in some Section 401 certiﬁcations that include requirements such as ﬁsh passage and ﬂow mandates, recreation measures, payment arrangements for oﬀsite programs, oﬀsite mitigation or studies, and other obligations not directly tied to a point source discharge from the project. The practical consequence is that a state certifying agency often ﬁnds itself in the role, whether it wants to or not, of being pressured to fulﬁll all the desires of other parties without mandatory conditioning authority by dumping various recommendations and suggestions into the Section 401 certiﬁcation. This approach can result in Section 401 certiﬁcations with conditions that are duplicative of other license conditions or conﬂict with other requirements or project purposes. Besides bypassing FERC’s role, this catch-all, backstop method of utilizing the Section 401 certiﬁcation also inhibits the ability of parties to negotiate in good faith through a license settlement process. At a time of rapidly growing electricity demand and pressing reinvestment needs to maintain dam infrastructure and generation units, the licensing process needs clear statutory lines: Section 401 should focus on discharges; broader programmatic measures should be considered and balanced through the FERC licensing process. Chelan PUD Comments on the Proposed Regulations 1. For consistency with Section 401, EPA should limit the scope of Section 401 decisions and conditions to assuring that discharges from federally licensed or permitted activities comply with CWA Sections 301, 302, 303, 306, and 307 and state water quality-related regulations for point source discharges. 6 16 U.S.C. § 803(a). 3 Chelan County PUD comments cont. Chelan PUD supports the proposed regulations, which describe the scope of the Section 401 certiﬁcation requirements as “limited to assuring that a discharge from a federal licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements.” 7 Further, it deﬁnes “discharge” as a “discharge from a point source into waters of the United States.” 8 The proposed scope of certiﬁcation properly aligns EPA’s rules with the CWA, which requires certiﬁcation only when an activity “may result in any discharge into the navigable waters,” and requires certiﬁcation only that “any such discharge will comply” with speciﬁed provisions of the CWA. Any applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity including, but not limited to, the construction or operation of facilities, which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters, shall provide the licensing or permitting agency a certiﬁcation from the State in which the discharge originates or will originate, or, if appropriate, from the interstate water pollution control agency having jurisdiction over the navigable waters at the point where the discharge originates or will originate, that any such discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of sections 1311, 1312, 1313, 1316, and 1317 of this title. 9 Section 401 does not require or authorize state water quality agencies to certify compliance for the entire activity being licensed or permitted under federal law—just the discharge that triggers the need for certiﬁcation. This distinction matters in practice because some state certifying authorities have used Section 401 to impose broad, programmatic, and multi- decade obligations on hydropower projects that are not tied to a point source discharge, and that overlap with or displace FERC’s role under the FPA. As EPA points out in the preamble to the Proposed Rule, there is a signiﬁcant role for states and tribes under the CWA, but there are also limits: “Section 401 envisions a robust State and Tribal role in the Federal licensing or permitting proceedings, including those in which local authority may otherwise be preempted by Federal law. Section 401 also places important limitations on how that role may be implemented to maintain an eﬃcient process, consistent with the overall cooperative federalism construct established by the CWA.” 10 Subsection (d) of CWA Section 401 authorizes the certifying authority to place conditions on any certiﬁcation, which become conditions on the federal license or permit: 7 91 Fed. Reg. 2023 (Jan. 16, 2026). 8 Ibid. 9 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). 10 91 Fed. Reg. 2010 (Jan. 15, 2026). 4 Chelan County PUD comments cont. Any certiﬁcation provided under this section shall set forth any eﬄuent limitations and other limitations, and monitoring requirements necessary to assure that any applicant for a Federal license or permit will comply with any applicable eﬄuent limitations and other limitations, under section 1311 or 1312 of this title [CWA Sections 301 and 302], standard of performance under section 1316 of this title [CWA Section 306], or prohibition, eﬄuent standard, or pretreatment standard under section 1317 of this title [CWA Section 307], and with any other appropriate requirement of State law set forth in such certiﬁcation, and shall become a condition on any Federal license or permit subject to the provisions of this section. 11 In implementing Section 401(d), some states have interpreted the phrase “any other appropriate requirement of State law” as authority to include wide-ranging conditions in WQCs so long as they relate – even loosely - to water, water management or resources that use water. EPA’s 2023 rule reinforced this expansive approach by stating it “defers to the relevant State and Tribe to deﬁne which of their state or Tribal provisions qualify as appropriate ‘state law’ or Tribal law for purposes of implementing section 401.” 12 EPA points out that the “best reading of the text of CWA section 401 limits the scope of certiﬁcation to “discharges” and not to the “activity.” Further EPA opines on the CWA’s relationships to discharges under Section 401(a)(2), where references to discharge are “clear indications that the subject of the entire CWA section 401 process – from certiﬁcation pursuant to CWA section 401(a)(1) to the neighboring jurisdiction process pursuant to CWA section 401(a)(2)—is focused on discharges, not the broader activity.” 13 EPA further highlights how amendments to the CWA in 1972 support EPA’s interpretation, speciﬁcally that Congress modiﬁed language from “activity” to discharge” when discussing the role of a certifying authority in determining compliance.14 The 1972 amendments to Section 401(a)(1) are as follows: Any applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity . . . which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters of the United States, shall provide the licensing or permitting agency a certiﬁcation from the State in which the discharge originates or will originate . . . that there is reasonable assurance, as determined by the State . . . that such activity will be conducted in a manner which will not violate applicable water quality standards that any such discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of sections 1311, 1312, 1313, 1316, and 1317 of this title. Congress clearly focused on discharges in its 1972 amendments. Chelan PUD agrees with and supports EPA’s Proposed Rule, which addresses the relationship of Section 401 11 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d) (emphasis added). 12 88 Fed. Reg. 66,604 (Sept. 27, 2023). 13 91 Fed. Reg. 2024 (Jan. 16, 2026). 14 Ibid. 5 Chelan County PUD comments cont. certiﬁcation to discharges as deﬁned in the 1972 amendments and makes clear that conditions must remain tethered to assuring compliance with applicable CWA provisions for the discharges that trigger certiﬁcation. 2. EPA should further clarify the scope of certiﬁcation in the context of hydropower projects. A discharge-focused 401 certiﬁcation matters for hydropower licensing. Many hydropower projects – particularly run-of-river facilities – do not “originate” 15 water quality conditions; they pass river water through turbines, ﬁshways, and or spillways and return it downstream. Water entering a reservoir or powerhouse may already fail to meet standards due to upstream agricultural runoﬀ, sediment, temperature, or other non-project sources. When Section 401 is treated as a broad waterbody restoration mandate, FERC license applicants face pressure to mitigate upstream or non-attributable conditions through operational constraints, oﬀsite projects, or ﬁnancial contributions. Those measures are beyond the statutory logic of certifying “any such discharge” from the federally licensed activity. EPA’s Proposed Rule should clarify the scope of certiﬁcation in the ﬁnal rule to prevent scenarios where hydropower projects must mitigate pollutants (such as heat, sediments and nitrogen) from upstream sources that are beyond their control. Further, it should be made clear that a certiﬁcation condition for a hydropower project cannot go beyond the scope of certiﬁcation by regulating the activity as a whole – for example, requiring ﬁsh passage facilities or minimum ﬂow requirements. Such requirements are addressed through the comprehensive FERC licensing process through the input of multiple federal and state agencies and balanced by FERC. Chelan PUD supports the deﬁnition proposed by NHA in its comment letter, speciﬁcally, the proposed regulations at Section 121.3 should be revised to state: The scope of a Clean Water Act section 401 certiﬁcation is limited to assuring that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements. Activities or eﬀects that are not within the scope of certiﬁcation include, but are not limited to: (a) Eﬀects caused by the presence of pollutants in a discharge that are not attributable to the discharge from the Federally licensed or permitted activity; (b) Eﬀects attributable to features of the Federally licensed or permitted activity other than the discharge; and (c) Eﬀects caused by the absence or reduction of a discharge, including eﬀects attributable to the diversion or impoundment of water. 3. EPA should clarify enforcement and conﬁrm FERC’s authority to address out-of-scope conditions and waiver. 15 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1) 6 Chelan County PUD comments cont. Section 401(d) provides that certiﬁcation conditions “shall become a condition on any Federal license or permit” subject to Section 401. 16 The CWA does not confer authority on other entities to enforce certiﬁcation conditions outside the federal license or permit. Instead, it states conditions “shall become a condition on any Federal. license or permit.” Conditions are enforceable after they become part of the federal license or permit and are tied to the federal license or permit. However, sometimes certifying authorities and third parties attempt to enforce certiﬁcation conditions themselves. To address this tactic, EPA should expressly provide in the ﬁnal rule that certiﬁcation conditions are enforceable only after incorporation into the federal license or permit, and then only by the federal licensing or permitting agency in the same manner and to the same extent as other license or permit conditions. Further, Chelan PUD supports EPA explicitly recognizing that a federal permitting or licensing authority like FERC can reject or decline to include certiﬁcation conditions that are outside the lawful scope of Section 401. A proactive recognition of this authority by EPA would be an important factor in supporting the relative roles of participants in the hydropower licensing process and facilitate any settlement negotiations. Finally, Chelan PUD supports NHA’s comments regarding FERC’s ability to determine that certiﬁcation has been waived where a certifying authority fails or refuses to act within the statutory timeframe, or where a denial rests on reasons outside the scope of certiﬁcation. Conclusion As EPA ﬁnalizes the rule, we urge the Agency to be explicit in ﬁnal regulatory text and guidance that 1) the discharge-based scope constraint applies to certiﬁcation conditions as well as certiﬁcation decisions; and 2) certifying authorities may not treat Section 401 as a “catch-all” backstop for broad, multi-decade settlement terms or non-discharge programmatic obligations that are better considered and balanced through the FERC licensing process under the FPA. Thank you for the opportunity to provide input. Sincerely, Alene Underwood Director, Natural Resources Chelan County PUD 16 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d). 7
